evals.prompt:
I'm about to build a job queue system in Go using Redis. Here are some articles I've gathered:

https://redis.io/docs/latest/develop/use/patterns/bulk-loading/
https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/hibiken/asynq
https://www.opsdash.com/blog/job-queues-in-go.html

Can you synthesise these for my use case? I specifically need to understand reliability guarantees and how to handle retries.
evals.expected_output: A synthesised document covering: key concepts for job queues in Go/Redis, consensus on reliability patterns, tensions between approaches (e.g. at-least-once vs at-most-once delivery), practical patterns for retries relevant to the user's goal, gotchas, and gaps. Should NOT be three separate summaries — must be cross-source synthesis tailored to the reliability/retry angle.
expectations: Output contains a 'mental model' or key concepts section with Go/Redis-specific concepts
Output addresses retry patterns specifically (user's explicit ask)
Output identifies at least one tension or trade-off (e.g. delivery guarantees, persistence options)
Output does NOT structure itself as 'Source 1 says... Source 2 says...' — it synthesises across sources
Output contains a practical patterns section relevant to the job queue use case
Output includes a gaps or watch-out section

evals.prompt:
I want to build a small RAG system. I don't know much about embeddings or vector DBs. Here's my reading list:

https://www.pinecone.io/learn/retrieval-augmented-generation/
https://simonwillison.net/2023/Oct/23/embeddings/
https://www.llamaindex.ai/blog/a-cheat-sheet-and-some-recipes-for-building-advanced-rag-21801f1579bc

My use case: I'm building a customer support bot that answers from a knowledge base of markdown docs. Keep it practical, I just need to get something working.
evals.expected_output: Synthesis tailored to: small-scale RAG for markdown docs / customer support. Should distil key concepts (embeddings, chunking, retrieval, augmentation), surface practical patterns for a simple implementation, flag common beginner mistakes, and identify gaps. Should be opinionated about where to start rather than covering all options.
expectations: Output explains chunking as a key concept (it's a common confusion point for RAG beginners)
Output is tailored to the customer support / markdown docs use case, not generic RAG theory
Output gives concrete recommended next steps the user can act on immediately
Output identifies at least one tension or trade-off (e.g. chunk size, embedding model choice)
Output does not just summarise each source in sequence
Use-case frame section restates the user's goal accurately

evals.prompt:
I'm learning how to set up observability for a FastAPI service — logs, metrics, traces. Never done this before. Here are some links I've been given:

https://opentelemetry.io/docs/languages/python/getting-started/
https://betterstack.com/community/guides/logging/python-logging-best-practices/
https://www.datadoghq.com/blog/python-logging-best-practices/

I'm deploying to Kubernetes and shipping logs/traces to Grafana stack (Loki + Tempo + Prometheus). Synthesise these for me.
evals.expected_output: Synthesis tailored to: FastAPI observability on Kubernetes with Grafana stack. Key concepts: the three pillars of observability, OpenTelemetry, structured logging. Practical patterns for FastAPI + Grafana stack. Tensions: between Python's logging module and OTEL, between structured and unstructured logs. Gaps: sources may not cover Kubernetes-specific concerns or Grafana integration specifically.
expectations: Output covers all three pillars (logs, metrics, traces) since that is the user's explicit scope
Output is tailored to Grafana stack (Loki/Tempo/Prometheus) not just generic observability
Output mentions OpenTelemetry as a key concept given the source provided
Output identifies at least one tension or trade-off between the approaches in the sources
Output contains a 'watch out for' or gotchas section
Output does not structure itself as source-by-source summaries
